25.text: Same dataset, same model family. The point is not what the model learns, but how different attention algorithms move data while producing the same answer.

26.text: We keep the run deterministic so the only meaningful comparison is between standard, online, and tiled attention implementations.

27.text: This lab mixes PyTorch training with NumPy attention kernels because the real teaching target is memory traffic, not autograd machinery.

29.text: NumPy is the right tool here because it lets the attention algorithm stay explicit and inspectable instead of disappearing behind fused kernels.

30.text: PyTorch still trains the tiny model, but the educational center of gravity is the handwritten attention kernels, not the training loop.

31.text: The learned model is just there to generate realistic tensors. The real lesson is how the same attention result can cost radically different amounts of memory movement.

32.text: Softmax is particularly important here because FlashAttention's big idea is not changing softmax, but reorganizing how it is computed and stored.

34.text: Fix the run so the algorithm comparison stays clean and reproducible.

35.text: NumPy's modern RNG, used for sampling during inference.

37.text: Float64 ensures the three attention implementations match to very high precision, we want to prove they compute the same thing.

42.text: Resolve dataset path relative to this script.

43.text: Download the names dataset if not already cached.

46.text: Download the names dataset from Karpathy's GitHub repo if it's not already cached locally.

50.text: Each line is one name, our training documents.

54.text: Collect every unique character across all names and sort them to create a deterministic character-to-ID mapping.

55.text: BOS token at the end of the character set; vocab = characters + BOS.

61.text: Model hyperparameters: intentionally small so the three attention implementations run quickly and we can compare their memory access counts meaningfully.

62.text: Each attention head works on head_dim = 4 dimensions.

70.text: Normalize by root-mean-square, no learned parameters.

74.text: Standard causal self-attention, used for training only. The NumPy versions below are for inference comparison.

77.text: Q, K, V, and output projections, the four weight matrices of attention.

82.text: Training forward pass uses PyTorch's optimized attention.

83.text: Extract batch size, sequence length, and embedding dimension from the input shape.

84.text: Reshape into multi-head format: (B, n_head, T, head_dim).

85.text: Project to keys and reshape into multi-head format, same as queries above.

86.text: Reshape value vectors into the same multi-head format. Unlike Q and K which compute scores, values carry the content attention retrieves.

87.text: Scaled dot-product scores between queries and keys.

88.text: Causal mask: prevent attending to future positions.

90.text: Softmax + value aggregation in one line, the "standard" way.

91.text: Recombine the independent head outputs into a single vector by projecting through the output weight matrix.

94.text: Transformer block with attention and MLP.

97.text: RMSNorm before attention stabilizes the input scale so the projection matrices see consistent activation magnitudes.

98.text: The causal self-attention sublayer, where each token gathers information from all previous positions via Q/K/V projections.

99.text: RMSNorm before the feed-forward network, ensuring the MLP receives well-scaled inputs regardless of what attention produced.

100.text: MLP up-projection: expand to 4x embedding dim.

101.text: MLP down-projection: back to embedding dim.

103.text: Block forward: attention + MLP with residual connections.

104.text: Add the attention output to the original input via a skip connection, so the layer can only refine, not overwrite.

105.text: Residual around MLP (inline: norm -> expand -> ReLU -> contract).

109.text: Full GPT model, identical architecture to Lab 03.

112.text: Learned dense vectors for each token in the vocabulary, mapping discrete IDs into continuous embedding space.

113.text: Learned dense vectors for each sequence position, telling the model where each token sits in the input.

114.text: RMSNorm applied to the combined embeddings before entering the transformer, stabilizing the first layer's input scale.

115.text: A sequence of transformer blocks stacked on top of each other, progressively refining the token representations.

117.text: Walk every submodule and apply the weight initialization function so all parameters start with well-scaled random values.

121.text: Only initialize Linear and Embedding layers with the small Gaussian. Other module types (like RMSNorm) have no learned weights.

124.text: Forward pass: embed, normalize, transform, predict.

126.text: Combine token and position embeddings, then normalize.

127.text: Feed the normalized embeddings through each transformer block in sequence, progressively building richer representations.

129.text: Final linear layer produces next-token logits.

135.text: Instantiate the PyTorch model for training.

137.text: Adam optimizer, same settings as other labs.

138.text: Train for 1000 gradient steps, which is enough for this small character-level model to learn basic name patterns.

141.text: Pick one training example per step, cycling through the shuffled dataset.

143.text: Truncate the token sequence to fit within the model's positional embedding table and context window.

146.text: Run the model on the input sequence to get next-token predictions at every position.

147.text: Compute the cross-entropy loss: how far the model's predicted probabilities are from the true next tokens.

148.text: Reset all parameter gradients to zero before computing the new backward pass.

149.text: Compute gradients of the loss with respect to every model parameter via backpropagation.

150.text: Reduce the learning rate linearly from its initial value toward zero over training, allowing fine-grained convergence at the end.

151.text: Write the decayed learning rate into every optimizer parameter group so the next step uses the updated value.

153.text: Apply the computed gradients to update all model parameters one step toward lower loss.

162.text: Extract all trained weights to NumPy, from here on, everything is explicit NumPy so we can count every memory access.

163.text: Gather embedding and head weights into a flat dictionary.

164.text: Pull each layer's attention weight matrices (Q, K, V, output) out of the PyTorch model and convert them to NumPy arrays.

165.text: Q, K, V, and output projection matrices.

166.text: Transpose to match NumPy's convention (input on rows, output on columns).

171.text: Numerically stable softmax in NumPy, subtract max to prevent overflow.

172.text: Raise e to each shifted logit value. The prior max-subtraction ensures the largest exponent is 0, preventing numerical overflow.

173.text: Divide each exponentiated value by the total so the output forms a proper probability distribution summing to 1.

176.text: RMSNorm in NumPy, matches the PyTorch version exactly.

186.text: The shared inference forward pass, identical computation, but the attention function is pluggable. This is how we compare the three algorithms with the same surrounding code.

189.text: Will hold memory operation counts from the last attention call.

190.text: Embed tokens + positions, then normalize, same as PyTorch forward pass.

191.text: Walk through each transformer layer in sequence, applying attention and MLP sublayers with residual connections.

192.text: Snapshot the current hidden state before attention modifies it. The residual connection adds this back, preserving the original signal.

193.text: Normalize activations before attention so the Q/K/V projections see inputs with consistent scale across layers.

194.text: Project to Q, K, V and reshape into multi-head format: (n_head, seq_len, head_dim). This is where the three attention implementations diverge.

197.text: Call the pluggable attention function, standard, online, or tiled. Returns output and memory stats.

198.text: Reshape attention output back to (seq_len, n_embd), apply output projection, add residual.

199.text: Snapshot the hidden state before the MLP modifies it. The residual path ensures the MLP can only add refinements.

200.text: Normalize before the feed-forward network so the MLP weights see well-scaled inputs regardless of what attention produced.

201.text: Multiply the normalized hidden state by the first MLP weight matrix, expanding to 4x width.

203.text: Final projection to vocabulary logits. Return logits and memory stats from the last layer's attention.

209.text: Algorithm 1: Standard attention. Materializes the full N*N attention matrix in HBM (slow GPU memory). This is what everyone learned first, and it's the memory bottleneck FlashAttention eliminates.

214.text: Count every read/write to "HBM" (slow memory) to make the cost explicit.

215.text: Read all Q and K vectors from HBM to compute scores.

216.text: The O(N^2) matrix: every query attends to every key. This is the expensive part to store.

217.text: Write the full N*N score matrix to HBM, this is the waste FlashAttention avoids.

218.text: Build the causal mask: upper triangle blocks future positions.

219.text: Replace future-position scores with negative infinity. After softmax, these become zero, enforcing the causal left-to-right constraint.

220.text: Read the N*N matrix back from HBM for softmax, a full round-trip through slow memory.

221.text: Row-wise softmax to get attention weights.

222.text: Write the N*N attention weights back to HBM.

223.text: Read back the weights AND values to compute the final weighted sum.

224.text: Matrix multiply: attention weights * values = output.

225.text: Write the output back to HBM. Total: 2 round-trips of the N*N matrix through slow memory.

232.text: Algorithm 2: Online softmax. Processes one key at a time, never materializing the N*N matrix. The foundation of FlashAttention, same output, far less memory traffic.

239.text: Track HBM operations, this algorithm has zero N*N writes.

240.text: Allocate output buffer, same shape as Q.

242.text: Iterate over each attention head independently, since heads share no state and can be processed in any order.

244.text: Online softmax begins by loading one query row at a time. That is why memory use stays linear instead of materializing the whole attention matrix.

245.text: Count the HBM read for this query.

247.text: The online softmax trick: maintain a running max, sum, and weighted output. No need to store all scores first.

248.text: Running sum of exp(scores) for softmax denominator.

249.text: Running weighted sum of values, the attention output, accumulated incrementally.

251.text: The inner loop streams over past keys and values only, matching the causal structure without ever storing an NxN score table.

252.text: Read one K and one V vector from HBM.

254.text: Record the HBM read cost for loading this key and value vector pair.

256.text: Dot the query vector with this key vector, scaled by sqrt(head_dim) to prevent softmax saturation in high dimensions.

257.text: Track the running maximum so softmax can be updated stably as more keys arrive instead of needing the full row upfront.

258.text: Correction factor: when the max changes, previous exponentials need rescaling. This is the key insight of online softmax, you can update the normalization incrementally.

259.text: Rescale the running sum and add the new score's contribution.

260.text: Accumulate the weighted output incrementally alongside the running normalization terms. This is the core online-softmax trick.

261.text: Update the running max for the next iteration.

263.text: Only the final normalized output row is written back. The intermediate attention probabilities never hit main memory.

264.text: Write only the final output to HBM, no N*N matrix ever touches slow memory.

272.text: Algorithm 3: Tiled attention (FlashAttention). The best of both worlds: processes BLOCKS of keys at once (vectorized like standard) but never writes the N*N matrix (memory-efficient like online). This is what runs on real GPUs.

280.text: Track HBM operations, expect fewer reads than online softmax (due to blocking) and same writes.

281.text: Column block size: how many keys to process at once.

282.text: Row block size: how many queries to process at once.

283.text: Allocate the output array with the same shape as Q to store attention results.

285.text: Iterate over each attention head independently, since heads do not interact during attention.

286.text: Tile the query dimension into blocks of size Br.

287.text: Handle the last block which may be smaller.

288.text: Read a block of query vectors from slow HBM memory into fast on-chip SRAM, where all the compute for this tile happens.

289.text: How many rows in this query block.

290.text: Count the HBM read, one block of queries, not one at a time.

292.text: Per-row running max for online softmax within this query block.

293.text: Running sum of exponentiated scores for each query row, used as the softmax denominator. Updated incrementally as new key blocks arrive.

294.text: Running weighted output for each query row, accumulated block by block. After all key blocks, dividing by the sum completes softmax.

296.text: Causal boundary: only attend to keys at or before the query positions.

297.text: Tile the key dimension into blocks of size Bc.

299.text: Read a block of key vectors from HBM into SRAM. Processing keys in blocks amortizes the memory transfer cost over many queries.

300.text: Read the corresponding block of value vectors alongside the keys, since both are needed to update the running output.

301.text: How many columns in this key block.

302.text: Count the K+V block read, amortized over block_cols positions.

304.text: Block matrix multiply: all queries against all keys in this block. This is where the parallelism comes from, much better than one-at-a-time.

307.text: Apply causal mask within this tile. Only positions where key <= query should contribute.

308.text: Walk through every query-key pair in the current tile to apply the causal mask where keys come after queries.

309.text: If the key position is after the query position, mask it out.

310.text: Replace causally invalid scores with a large negative value so softmax gives them zero weight, matching the standard attention mask.

313.text: Online softmax update across blocks, same math as Algorithm 2, but operating on entire blocks at once.

314.text: New running max: element-wise maximum of old max and this block's max.

315.text: Correction factor for previous blocks when the max increases.

316.text: Exponentiate scores relative to the new max.

318.text: Update running sum: rescale old sum, add new block's contribution.

319.text: Update running output: rescale old output, add new weighted values. This is the block version of the online softmax accumulation.

320.text: Update the running max for the next block.

322.text: Normalize by the final sum, completes the softmax computation for this query block.

323.text: Write only the final output block to HBM, the N*N matrix never existed in slow memory.

331.text: Pick one name from the dataset as test input.

332.text: How long is our test sequence.

334.text: Feed the same test input through standard, online, and tiled attention to verify all three produce identical results.

335.text: Online softmax should produce identical results, it's mathematically exact.

336.text: Tiled attention uses block-level operations, tiny floating-point differences possible.

339.text: Online softmax should match to machine precision (1e-10).

340.text: Tiled allows slightly more tolerance due to different summation order.

343.text: Print the actual numerical differences, should be negligibly small.

345.text: Hard assertion: if any algorithm diverges, the implementation is wrong.

346.text: The whole point: same math, different memory access patterns, identical results.

351.text: Now compare how many times each algorithm reads/writes slow HBM memory.

353.text: Print stats for all three algorithms with explanatory notes.

362.text: Pull the read count and write count out of each algorithm's stats dictionary so we can compare memory traffic side by side.

363.text: Total memory operations = reads + writes. Standard attention dominates because of the N*N matrix.

366.text: Calculate the theoretical HBM traffic for each algorithm using the sequence length and head dimensions for the summary table.

367.text: Summary of the exact write counts. Across all heads, standard attention writes 2*n_head*N*N score/probability elements while tiled and online write only n_head*N*d output elements; per head that is 2*N*N vs N*d.

394.text: Temperature 0.5 for more focused name generation.

396.text: Generate 20 sample names using the tiled (FlashAttention) attention algorithm.

399.text: Generate up to block_size tokens per name.

400.text: Full forward pass with tiled attention for each step.

401.text: Divide logits by temperature before softmax. Lower temperature sharpens the distribution toward the most likely token.

402.text: Convert the temperature-scaled logits into a probability distribution via softmax for sampling the next token.

403.text: Sample from the distribution using NumPy's RNG.

406.text: Extend the token sequence for the next step.

407.text: Convert token ID back to character.

408.text: Display each generated name on its own line so we can visually inspect the model's learned character patterns.
